Those whom God pardons must know what their sin deserved. “Let them go forward as they are,” this expressed God's displeasure. Though he promises to fulfill his covenant with Abraham by giving them Canaan, yet he denies them the signs of his presence that they had previously enjoyed. The people mourned for their sin. Of all the bitter results and consequences of sin, true penitents most lament and dread God’s departure from them. Canaan itself would not be a pleasant land without the Lord's presence. Those who gave up valuables to continue in sin could do no less than lay aside valuables as a sign of sorrow and shame for it.

Moses took the tabernacle and pitched it outside the camp. This seems to have been a temporary building set up for worship, and where he judged disputes among the people. The people watched after him; they were very desirous of being at peace with God, and concerned to know what would happen. The cloudy pillar which had withdrawn from the camp when it was defiled by idolatry now returned. If our hearts go toward God to meet him, he will graciously come to meet us.

Moses is very earnest with God. Thus, through Christ’s intercession, we are not only saved from ruin but become entitled to everlasting happiness. Observe how he pleads here. We find grace in God's sight if we find grace in our hearts to guide and quicken us in the way of our duty. Moses speaks as one who dreaded the thought of going forward without the Lord’s presence. God’s gracious promises and mercy toward us should not only encourage our faith but also stir up our earnestness in prayer. Observe how he succeeds. See, in a type, Christ's intercession, which he always lives to make for all who come to God through him; and that it is not by anything in those for whom he intercedes. Moses then asks to see God’s glory and is heard in that also. A full revelation of the glory of God would overwhelm even Moses himself. Man is insignificant and unworthy of it; weak, and could not endure it; guilty, and could not but dread it. The merciful display made in Christ Jesus alone can be borne by us. The Lord granted what would abundantly satisfy. God’s goodness is his glory; and he wants us to know him by the glory of his mercy more than by the glory of his majesty. Upon the rock there was a suitable place for Moses to view the goodness and glory of God. The rock in Horeb was typical of Christ, the Rock of refuge, salvation, and strength. Happy are those who stand upon this Rock. The cleft may be an emblem of Christ as smitten, crucified, wounded, and slain. What follows denotes the imperfect knowledge of God in the present state, even as revealed in Christ; for this, when compared with the heavenly sight of him, is like seeing a man that has gone by, whose back only can be seen. God in Christ, as he is, even the fullest and brightest displays of his glory, grace, and goodness, are reserved to another state.
